# Questions in Livestream 221

Live Stream #221: The Concise Fun Notes Edition

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=488" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:08</a> | Given Akkadian and Amorite, can Abraham's lines in Genesis 12:11-13 be roughly reconstructed or pronounced? | Not yet; he says the Amorite evidence is still too small, especially for alphabetic Amorite. | He says the available Amorite material gives some syntax and vocabulary, but only from a very small corpus, more like a phrasebook than a full language resource. Because the evidence is cuneiform and alphabetic Amorite remains uncertain, he does not think Genesis 12:11-13 can yet be responsibly back-translated or pronounced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:55</a> | Does formal government censorship still occur in the West? | He says the West has more often used informal censorship, especially private industry cooperating with the state. | He argues that Western censorship has often worked indirectly, with government pressure routed through guilds, publishers, banks, platforms, or other private intermediaries. His examples contrast that model with direct state control and frame modern platform or financial-service pressure as a continuation of older informal patterns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=974" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:14</a> | Does 1 Kings 9:26 suggest that yam suf there is really a reed sea rather than the Gulf of Aqaba proper? | Yes; he says the marshy northern setting fits that wording. | He reads 1 Kings 9:26 as referring to a reed-sea environment at Ezion-geber near the north end of the Gulf of Aqaba. He distinguishes this from a bare saltwater beach, explaining that reeds require brackish marsh conditions created where freshwater runoff mixes with salt water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=1303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:43</a> | How should Ezra 4:4-7:23-24 be understood: is Artaxerxes Cambyses II, or did Artaxerxes stop the wall work he had authorized? | He leans toward the latter reading. | He reads the Artaxerxes in the passage as Artaxerxes I and says the surrounding peoples slandered the Jews as rebellious after permission had been given. On that reading, the king later retracted permission and stopped the work by force rather than the name being a disguised reference to Cambyses II. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=1583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:23</a> | What are your thoughts on the identity of Darius the Mede? | He treats the text as presenting a real ruler over the empire and works through the passage as a historical problem rather than dismissing it. | He rejects easy answers that make Darius merely a governor or a literary invention, because Daniel depicts him as ruling broadly and appointing officials across the kingdom. He considers the problem historically difficult, raises the possibility of an epithet or debated identification, and connects the issue to how Ezra and Daniel use Persian royal names. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=2041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:01</a> | What are your thoughts on the ISV translation? | He has only sampled parts of it and does not want to give a sweeping review without living with it longer. | He says the ISV seems willing to avoid some traditional renderings, which he appreciates, but also uses modern translation practices that remain debated. Because he has not used a hard copy over time for study and casual reading, he limits his judgment to those impressions rather than reviewing it fully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=2281" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:01</a> | If Nabonidus was Belshazzar's father, why does Daniel refer to Nebuchadnezzar as Belshazzar's father? | He says "father" can be broad in biblical Hebrew, and Nabonidus may have had a family tie to Nebuchadnezzar by marriage. | He explains that biblical Hebrew can use father and son language for ancestors or descendants more broadly than direct parentage. He also says there may have been a marriage connection between Nabonidus and Nebuchadnezzar's house, so Daniel need not be claiming a strict patrilineal father-son relationship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=2420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:20</a> | What book or books should a total beginner use to start learning Hebrew? | He recommends an introductory grammar, especially Paige Kelly's textbook. | He recommends Paige Kelly's _Biblical Hebrew: An Introductory Grammar_, especially with the exercise book and answer key, calling it useful both for introductory learning and later reference. He also advises a beginner to get help with Hebrew vowels and consonants first, since that initial step is the part most unlike English. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=2761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:01</a> | Why did Moses's mother place him in the river basket? | He says she hoped an Egyptian would pity and adopt him, not that she knew Pharaoh's daughter would appear. | He says Moses had become old enough to make hiding him dangerous, so his mother placed him where an Egyptian might find and pity him. He adds that Egyptian women had broad adoption rights, making adoption by a compassionate Egyptian a plausible hope, while Pharaoh's daughter is treated as a later title rather than something Moses's mother could have planned around. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=3092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:32</a> | When was the Bible written? | He calls it an anthology written over a long span, with the individual books far earlier than the first complete bound Bibles. | He distinguishes the writing of individual biblical books from the later binding of the Bible as one codex. In his summary, the books span from the Late Bronze Age through the end of the first century AD, while complete bound Bibles appear much later after the codex form made one-volume collections practical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=3587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:47</a> | Is the claim accurate that Codex Sinaiticus was written by or on behalf of Origen? | He says Sinaiticus is contemporary with Origen, but that authorship or sponsorship claim is hard to prove. | He allows that Codex Sinaiticus belongs roughly in Origen's era, so the chronological overlap is not impossible. But he says proving that it was produced by Origen or on his behalf is a much stronger claim than the evidence can easily support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=3739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:19</a> | What are some good lay resources for learning ancient Mesopotamian and Egyptian history? | He points to Oxford reference works and especially Ian Shaw for Egypt. | For Egypt, he recommends Ian Shaw's Oxford handbook as a strong beginner reference and notes that some older works are now dated. For Mesopotamia, he is less definite but points generally to Oxford-style reference works as the kind of resource a lay reader should look for. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=4328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:08</a> | What are your thoughts on the Jerusalem Step Street or Pilgrim Road underground excavation? | He does not have strong settled views but regards the excavation itself as interesting and worthwhile. | He does not present a firm interpretation of the Pilgrim Road excavation, but he treats the site as archaeologically interesting and worth following. His answer stays cautious, distinguishing enthusiasm for the excavation from confidence about specific claims attached to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=4736" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:56</a> | Who was president of Egypt when the Qurna evictions happened? | He says Egypt still had a monarchy then; the evictions began around the 1940s before Nasser. | He corrects the framing by saying the early Qurna evictions were not under an Egyptian president because Egypt still had a king. He places the beginning of the process in the 1940s and says that is before the later Nasser-era republic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=4979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:59</a> | What is going on in Exodus 33:18-22 when God shows Moses his grace? | He treats the passage as centering on God's glory and God's sovereign choice to show grace. | He reads the scene as Moses asking to see God's glory and God responding by revealing his goodness, mercy, and grace on his own terms. The point is not grace as a detachable concept, but God's self-disclosure and sovereign choice to reassure Moses while still limiting what Moses can see. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=5358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:18</a> | Could you give a brief outline of how some of the ten plagues played out in reality? | He sketches them as intensified natural flood and weather events used by God in extraordinary ways. | He frames the plagues as real-world phenomena tied to Egyptian environmental patterns, especially flooding, disease, insects, and weather, but intensified and timed as divine acts. His answer does not reduce them to ordinary nature; it presents natural mechanisms as the medium through which the extraordinary judgment occurred. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=5571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:51</a> | Have you ever been interested in Warhammer 40k, and do you like it? | No; he says he never got into it, even though he had friends who did. | He says friends were interested in Warhammer and related war games, but the setting never became one of his own hobbies. He gives no critique of the franchise beyond saying it simply did not catch his interest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=5741" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:41</a> | Are any Egyptian dances known today that likely go back to antiquity, and does their music survive? | He says no clear continuity survives for either the dances or the music. | He says ancient Egyptian visual evidence can show that dance and music existed, but it does not preserve a direct modern dance tradition or the melodies themselves. Without reliable continuity, he does not identify any surviving modern Egyptian dance or music as traceable back to antiquity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=6072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:12</a> | Does 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1 show progressive revelation? | He says they are the same event viewed through different lenses, not really progressive revelation. | He treats the two passages as complementary descriptions of the same census episode: one focuses on God's anger and permission, while the other identifies Satan as the immediate inciter. For him, that is a difference in narrative lens and causation, not a later doctrine replacing an earlier one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=6510" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:30</a> | Have you read Garrett's commentary on Exodus 4 and the bridegroom of blood episode? | No; he says he has not read Garrett on that passage. | He answers that he has not read Garrett's treatment, then gives his own brief reading of the episode. In that reading, the issue is Moses returning to lead Israel while failing to circumcise his own son under the Abrahamic covenant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=6884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:44</a> | What is your view of sacrificing the red heifer in preparation for a third temple? | He says it would be necessary if temple service were actually going to resume. | He says a red heifer sacrifice would be required for purification if people were genuinely preparing to restart temple service. His answer is procedural rather than predictive: without that purification element, a functioning third-temple service would not be ready. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=6953" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:53</a> | Is it true that only eight adequately qualified red heifers have ever been found or used? | He says the tradition is that only eight were used, but probably more have been found. | He distinguishes between the rabbinic or traditional count of heifers actually used and the broader question of how many suitable animals may have existed. He thinks the count of eight refers to used heifers, not necessarily every qualified red heifer ever found. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:16</a> | How do you tell which commands are for Christians generally and which were specifically for Israel? | He points to covenant context, with temple and ritual laws tied to Israel while moral commands are reiterated more broadly. | He says the interpreter should ask where a command sits in the covenant structure and whether it is tied to Israel's temple, priesthood, sacrifices, or national life. Commands reiterated in broader moral teaching apply differently than ritual or civil commands bound to Israel's covenant administration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:05</a> | Is there any good reason to date Daniel to the second century? | He says the usual case depends too much on anti-prophecy assumptions and does not fit the linguistic evidence well. | He argues that much of the late-date case assumes Daniel could not contain accurate prophecy, then reasons backward from that assumption. He also says the linguistic evidence, including Daniel's Aramaic, does not fit a simple second-century composition as well as critics often claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:27</a> | Why do critics hate the book of Daniel? | He says Daniel draws hostility because its prophecies look fulfilled. | He says Daniel creates pressure for critics because its prophetic material appears to match later historical events. In his framing, that is why many critical approaches try to move the book later rather than treat the prophecies as genuinely predictive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:08</a> | If you could find one original Bible manuscript, which one would you choose? | He would choose Exodus because it would resolve major textual and linguistic questions. | He chooses Exodus because an autograph would clarify important textual questions and give direct evidence for early Hebrew usage. The value for him is not sentimental only; it would materially affect debates about the text and its language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:34</a> | Are there any ancient documents for which we do have autographs? | Yes; he says some less-copied documents such as treaties survive in autograph form. | He says autograph survival is unlikely for heavily copied literary works, but it is possible for administrative or diplomatic documents. Treaties are his example of the kind of ancient text where the original inscribed or written artifact can survive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=7886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:26</a> | Can the clan view date the flood to the Mesopotamian Early Dynastic period archaeologically? | No; he says honorary transposition and archaeology prevent that kind of precise flood dating. | He says the clan view does not allow the flood to be pinned neatly to a Mesopotamian Early Dynastic archaeological horizon. Because names and chronology can be honorifically transposed and the archaeology does not provide that precision, he rejects using the theory to date the flood that tightly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=8100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:00</a> | Were Israel's religious practices different in the Late Bronze Age than in the first century, and is there a resource on that? | Yes; he points especially to changes in temple and tabernacle practice over time. | He says Israelite practice changed substantially between the tabernacle, temple, exile, Second Temple, and first-century contexts. The best resource route, in his answer, is to study temple and tabernacle practice historically rather than assuming one static Israelite ritual system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=8393" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:53</a> | How would people cast lots in ancient Israel? | He describes lots as dice-like objects, including Urim and Thummim-style lots and throwing-stick lots. | He describes casting lots as a physical divination process using objects comparable to dice, marked stones, or sticks. He includes the Urim and Thummim as one biblical form and says the outcome was read from how the objects landed or were drawn. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=8730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:30</a> | Was the Masoretic Text altered to make prophecies about Jesus less obvious? | He rejects that as a conspiracy theory and prefers ordinary textual transmission problems as the explanation. | He says the evidence does not support a coordinated Jewish alteration of the Masoretic Text to hide Jesus. Where differences exist between textual traditions, he explains them through normal copying, transmission, and textual-family issues rather than an anti-Christian conspiracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=8895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:15</a> | What does Joseph's Egyptian name mean? | He tentatively parses it as something like "he is said to be the living one." | He gives the answer cautiously because the Egyptian name is difficult, but he tentatively parses it along the lines of "he is called" or "he is said to be" plus "the living one." The answer is presented as a provisional philological reconstruction, not a settled translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:42</a> | If God sustains creation but is contained in his temple, does sacred space theology result in two gods? | No; he explains it through an essence-energies distinction. | He says sacred-space theology does not require two gods because God's essence and God's active presence can be distinguished. God can sustain all things by his energies while also locating his special presence in the temple without dividing deity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9212" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:32</a> | Is the essence-energies distinction the same as Eastern Orthodox teaching, or is it found in Protestant theology too? | He says it is developed especially in Eastern Orthodoxy, though Western churches can still draw on it. | He identifies the distinction as especially developed in Eastern Orthodox theology, but he does not treat it as unusable by Protestants. His point is that Western Christians can still use the conceptual distinction when it helps explain biblical sacred-space language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9400" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:40</a> | Were fly and lice invasions common in ancient Egypt? | Yes; he says both were persistent problems, with lice attested as far back as predynastic contexts. | He says flies and lice were ordinary and persistent realities in Egypt, not exotic inventions of the plague narrative. He notes lice evidence very early in Egyptian contexts and treats the biblical plagues as intensifying familiar problems rather than introducing unknown phenomena. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:11</a> | What is the difference between Eastern Orthodox energy ideas and New Thought? | He says New Thought treats energy as manipulable by people, while Orthodox energy remains under God's sovereign control. | He contrasts New Thought-style energy with Orthodox divine energies by control and agency. In New Thought, humans manipulate impersonal energy for desired outcomes, while in Orthodox theology God's energies remain personal divine action and are not something creatures command. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9608" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:08</a> | Is the essence-energies distinction a contradiction of divine simplicity? | No; he says it does not contradict divine simplicity. | He says the distinction is intended to preserve both God's simplicity and the reality of God's operations toward creation. It does not split God into parts, because the distinction is not between separate divine substances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:10</a> | Does the essence-energies distinction apply to God binding himself to the sacraments without being bound by them? | He is not sure and says he has not yet received a good answer from Eastern Orthodox interlocutors. | He declines to give a confident answer and says he has asked Eastern Orthodox conversation partners about that kind of sacramental application without receiving an explanation he finds satisfactory. The transcript supports the uncertainty rather than a firm doctrinal conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:01</a> | If God is non-physical, can his essence still be in the temple without becoming spatial? | He argues spiritual realities can still have place-like or spatial properties without becoming physical. | He argues that non-physical does not necessarily mean non-spatial in every sense. Spiritual realities can be located or associated with place without being material bodies, so temple presence need not make God's essence physical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:47</a> | Does Gavin Ortlund's view of Protestantism as a continual renewal movement resonate with you? | Yes; he says Protestantism remains valid when it keeps reforming toward Christian maturity. | He agrees with the idea of Protestantism as continual reform when that means moving toward maturity, order, and greater faithfulness. He does not frame reform as change for its own sake, but as an ongoing correction toward what Christian life should be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=9982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:22</a> | What is Dr. Falk's denomination? | He declines to specify and answers only that he is Christian. | He intentionally avoids naming a denomination and gives only the broader answer that he is Christian. The expanded support is therefore limited: the transcript records a boundary-setting answer rather than a denominational explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10016" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:56</a> | What does continual reforming mean? | He describes it as continually reforming toward a mature, properly ordered Christian life. | He explains continual reforming as an ongoing movement toward maturity, not endless instability. The aim is for the Christian or church to become better ordered and more faithful over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10149" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:09</a> | Are hermits sinning by being hermits? | No, but he says hermits lose the benefits of Christian community. | He does not call hermit life inherently sinful, but he says isolation comes with real losses because Christian formation normally happens in community. His concern is deprivation of fellowship and accountability rather than a blanket condemnation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:56</a> | Why should omnipresence be thrown out? | He says full omnipresence creates theological problems by collapsing sacred and profane space. | He argues against a version of omnipresence in which God is equally present everywhere in the same way, because that undermines biblical distinctions between sacred and profane space. His concern is not God's knowledge or power, but the loss of meaningful localized divine presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10635" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:15</a> | When people say God is omnipresent, is that a confusion of God himself or just of the terms they use? | He thinks it usually reflects confusion about God rather than merely sloppy wording. | He says the issue is usually more than terminology, because many people really do mean that God is present everywhere in the same way. He thinks that idea conflicts with how Scripture treats God's special presence in holy places. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:29</a> | Are Old Testament texts about God's limited knowledge more literal than anthropomorphic? | He says he has not seen a text like that which could not be otherwise explained, but would need a specific example. | He says he would need an actual passage before deciding, but he has not encountered an Old Testament limited-knowledge text that could not be explained another way. The answer remains conditional because no specific text was supplied. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=10853" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:53</a> | Is omnipresence a child of Greek or Western thought? | No; he says it is more associated with Eastern and pantheistic thought than Greek thought. | He rejects the idea that omnipresence is simply a Greek or Western inheritance. In his answer, the concept has stronger affinities with Eastern or pantheistic patterns of thought than with classical Greek influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:22</a> | How did Eastern thought influence early Christianity and the church fathers? | He says direct influence was limited, even though trade put Eastern ideas in contact with the Mediterranean world. | He says Eastern ideas could travel through trade and contact, but he does not see strong evidence for deep direct influence on early Christianity or the church fathers. The answer separates possible exposure from demonstrable theological dependence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:56</a> | How credible is a mythicist claim dating the New Testament to the second century by stylometry and anachronisms? | He rejects it, citing early manuscript and patristic evidence and calling stylometry unreliable for that claim. | He says a second-century New Testament claim fails against early manuscript and patristic evidence that already presupposes the texts. He also distrusts stylometry for making such a large historical claim, especially when alleged anachronisms can often be explained otherwise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:33</a> | Do we know which specific Jewish group Philo of Alexandria or Crispus belonged to? | He says no precise faction is known in that case. | He does not identify Philo or Crispus with a specific Jewish sect or party. The supported answer is simply that the evidence is not precise enough to assign them confidently to a known faction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:56</a> | Were Seth-her-khepeshef and Amun-her-khepeshef different sons of Ramesses II? | He thinks they are the same person using different names while testing a royal religious agenda. | He says the names likely refer to the same royal son, with the divine element in the name shifting from Seth to Amun. He connects that to Ramesses II experimenting with or signaling religious-political priorities through naming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:49</a> | Did Pharisees or Sadducees exist in Nero's court? | He allows that Pharisees are possible, but says Sadducees would not fit that setting. | He says Pharisees could plausibly appear in a wider imperial or diaspora context, but Sadducees were closely tied to the Jerusalem temple establishment. Because of that institutional connection, he does not think Sadducees in Nero's court make sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11864" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:44</a> | If Yahweh resembles descriptions of Israel's neighbors' gods, is that the Lord meeting people in their culture? | He says some similarities are expected, but warns against reducing them to identity through parallelomania. | He says shared ancient Near Eastern language and imagery can be expected because Israel communicated in its cultural setting. But he warns that parallels do not prove identity or borrowing in a flattening sense, and he labels that overreach as parallelomania. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=11976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:36</a> | Do we know which form of Judaism Nero's wife Poppaea adhered to? | He says he does not know and treats it as outside his wheelhouse. | He does not claim enough knowledge to identify Poppaea with a particular Jewish group. The answer is deliberately limited to uncertainty rather than speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:03</a> | Were there Gnostic Jews? | Yes; he says Gnosticism can adapt to many religions and existed in Jewish forms. | He says Gnosticism is flexible and can attach itself to different religious frameworks, including Jewish ones. So Jewish Gnostic forms are possible and historically real, even if not every group called Jewish should be collapsed into the same category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12262" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:22</a> | Are Mandaeans remnants of John the Baptist's followers? | No; he identifies them as a later Zoroastrian Gnostic sect, not John the Baptist's followers. | He rejects the claim that Mandaeans preserve a direct community of John the Baptist's followers. In his answer they are better understood as a later Gnostic movement with Zoroastrian connections that reuses John the Baptist material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:40</a> | What is the name of the book on the Mandaeans? | He points to work by Kulp and Schenke and notes that the books may be hard to find and may not be translated. | He names scholarship associated with Kulp and Schenke, while warning that the relevant works may be difficult to obtain and may not be available in translation. The practical answer is therefore a research pointer, not a simple current popular title. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12527" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:47</a> | Who was the person on the Mose stela, and can he be linked with Moses? | He says the name is common in Egypt and the person on the stela is not the biblical Moses. | He says the Egyptian name Mose or Mes is common enough that it cannot identify the biblical Moses by itself. The person on the stela belongs to a different historical context and should not be linked to the Exodus figure merely because of the name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12625" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:25</a> | Do we know what scroll was found in Josiah's day? | He says Deuteronomy is the most likely answer. | He identifies Deuteronomy as the most likely candidate for the book or scroll found in Josiah's reform narrative. The answer is framed as probability from the content and reform context rather than a claim that the text explicitly names the scroll. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=12947" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:47</a> | Is it normal that the Veloca Rabbit channel link has no content? | No; he says the publisher retracted the music content, so that link is no longer good. | He says the empty channel is not the intended state of the link. The problem is that the publisher pulled back or retracted the music content, leaving the referenced channel no longer useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=13209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:09</a> | Why not self-publish the audio project? | He says self-publishing would not solve their distribution and monetization problems, especially from Canada. | He says the obstacle is not simply making the audio available, but handling platform access, distribution, payment, and monetization. Self-publishing from Canada would still run into the same gatekeeping and infrastructure problems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=13518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:18</a> | Why don't artists just create their own publishers? | He says access and gatekeeping are the problem, not lack of desire. | He says creating a publisher does not automatically create market access, distribution networks, payment rails, or platform cooperation. The difficulty is the existing gatekeeping structure, so artist-owned publishers alone would not solve the practical problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=13749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:09</a> | Was your visual novel already released, or is it still coming later? | It is planned for later release. | He says the visual novel had not yet been released and was still planned for a later release. The transcript support is limited to project-status clarification rather than details about content or date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=14401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:01</a> | Are there any serious options on hell besides annihilationism, eternal conscious torment, and universalism? | Yes; he says there are several additional models beyond that usual three-part list. | He says the common three-option framing is too narrow and that Christian discussions of hell include other serious models. His answer opens the taxonomy beyond annihilationism, eternal conscious torment, and universalism rather than choosing one in that moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EYAWYyFXlT8?t=14590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:10</a> | Is annihilationism just ceasing to exist, and therefore more merciful? | He says no; in his description, annihilationism still involves a period of torment before final destruction. | He says annihilationism should not be reduced to painless nonexistence. In the version he describes, the lost undergo punishment or torment before final destruction, so its supposed mercy depends on more than simply ceasing to exist. |
